text: The surgeon begins posterior rectal dissection at the sacral promontory, entering the presacral space and identifying the proper rectal fascia and presacral fascia. Dissection proceeds in an avascular plane between the proper rectal and parietal pelvic fascia, moving superiorly to the peritoneum at the rectovesical pouch. The low posterior dissection starts at the fourth sacral vertebra, cutting the retrosacral fascia to access the retrorectal space. Waldeyer's fascia is visible covering the floor of the retrorectal space. In the low lateral dissection, the proper rectal fascia is detached from Waldeyer's fascia, which covers the levator ani muscle.

The surgeon performs low anterior dissection by sectioning the non-villiers fascia at the base of the prostate, which adheres to the prosthetic capsule. The dissection proceeds posteriorly to the non-villiers fascia, identifying and cutting the rectal efferences of the inferior hypogastric plexus to access the levator ani muscles. The rectum is fully mobilized below the prostate, with Waldeyer's fascia covering the anococcygeal raphe, which is sectioned. The horizontal portion of the rectum is detached, leading to complete mobilization up to the puborectalis muscles at the anorectal junction. The rectum is transected with an endogia stapler approximately three to four centimeters from the anal verge. The specimen is retracted from the pelvis, exposing the pelvic floor, rectal stump, levator ani muscles, neurovascular bundle, and inferior hypogastric plexus. Nerve integrity is checked, and a standard stapled colorectal anastomosis is performed.

The surgeon performs a high anterior dissection in front of the de Noveles fascia, which thickens and adheres to the prostate at its base. The fascia is sectioned, and the dissection shifts posteriorly to contact the proper rectal fascia, aiming to fully mobilize the rectum anteriorly. The cut edge of the de Noveles fascia adhering to the prostate is visible. The rectal deferent branches of the inferior hypogastric plexus are sectioned to access the levator ani muscles. The rectal sacral fascia is identified and cut to enter the retrorectal space, and the last posterior attachment of the proper rectal fascia to the Waldeyer's fascia is severed.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic sectioning of the puborectalis muscle on the left side, posterolaterally, to facilitate dissection in the perineal region and achieve a safe circumferential margin. The laparoscopic approach provides enhanced magnification for superior visualization of the sectioning area.

The surgeon performs an intersphincteric dissection between the internal and external anal sphincters, starting at the dentate line and extending laterally on the left side to include the deep part of the external anal sphincter. The rectum is mobilized anteriorly, achieving a distal margin of at least one centimeter, confirmed by frozen sections. The specimen is extracted transanally using a pull-through technique. After preparing the mesorectum and checking the resection area, a standard end-to-end Park's manual colorectal anastomosis is performed. The postoperative view shows the abdomen with a temporary loop ileostomy. An endoscopic view reveals the anastomosis located one centimeter south-left from the anocutaneous line, observed six months post-surgery.

The surgeon sections the thickened Waldeyer's fascia posterolaterally to expose the fibers of the Levatorani muscle. Using a laparoscopic approach, the Levatorani is sectioned under direct vision to achieve a large safe circumferential margin, revealing the ischorectal adipose tissue. The posterior levator is cut in front of the coccyx, and on the left side, where the tumor invades the levator, it is detached from the internal obturator fascia. The rectum remains attached to the vagina at the site of tumoral invasion. A large circumferential incision is made to remove the sphincters, mirroring the abdominal dissection.

The surgeon resects the infiltrated posterior vaginal wall en bloc with the specimen, which is temporarily extracted. The levator ani and posterior vaginal wall are included in the resection, while the proper rectal fascia remains intact. The final postoperative view shows the perineum closed primarily.
